title: Calcium blocks iron absorption
detail: Calcium significantly reduces non-heme iron absorption when taken together. This is clinically relevant for people treating iron deficiency.
instruction: Take iron and calcium at least 2 hours apart. Take iron on an empty stomach with vitamin C for best absorption.
mechanism: Calcium competes with iron for the same intestinal transport proteins (DMT1).

title: Calcium reduces zinc absorption
detail: High calcium intake can reduce zinc absorption by up to 50% when taken simultaneously.
instruction: Separate calcium and zinc by at least 2 hours. Take zinc with a small amount of food.
mechanism: Competitive inhibition at intestinal absorption sites.

title: Calcium and magnesium compete for absorption
detail: High-dose calcium supplementation can reduce magnesium absorption. Ratio matters — aim for 2:1 calcium to magnesium.
instruction: Maintain a 2:1 calcium to magnesium ratio. Consider taking them at different times if doses are high.
mechanism: Shared intestinal transport and competition for cellular uptake.

title: Zinc depletes copper over time
detail: Long-term high-dose zinc supplementation (>40mg/day) can cause copper deficiency by blocking copper absorption. Even moderate doses need monitoring.
instruction: If taking zinc long-term, add 1-2mg copper daily. Keep zinc under 40mg/day unless directed by a doctor.
mechanism: Zinc induces metallothionein in intestinal cells which binds copper and prevents absorption.

title: Vitamin D3 works best with K2
detail: Vitamin D3 increases calcium absorption. Vitamin K2 (MK-7) directs that calcium into bones and away from arteries. Taking D3 without K2 long-term may increase arterial calcification risk.
instruction: Take vitamin K2 (MK-7, 100-200mcg) with your vitamin D3. Both are fat-soluble — take with a meal containing fat.
mechanism: K2 activates osteocalcin and matrix Gla protein which regulate calcium deposition.

title: Vitamin C dramatically improves iron absorption
detail: Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) converts non-heme iron to its more absorbable ferrous form and can increase iron absorption by 2-6x.
instruction: Take iron with 200-500mg vitamin C for maximum absorption. Take on an empty stomach for best results.
mechanism: Ascorbic acid reduces ferric iron (Fe3+) to ferrous iron (Fe2+) which is more readily absorbed.

title: Black pepper dramatically increases turmeric absorption
detail: Piperine in black pepper increases curcumin bioavailability by up to 2000%. Without it, most curcumin passes through unabsorbed.
instruction: Always take turmeric/curcumin with black pepper extract (piperine) or choose a supplement that includes it. Look for 'BioPerine' on the label.
mechanism: Piperine inhibits intestinal and hepatic metabolism of curcumin, dramatically extending its bioavailability.

title: Magnesium is required to activate Vitamin D
detail: Magnesium is a cofactor for the enzymes that convert vitamin D into its active form. Magnesium deficiency can make vitamin D supplementation ineffective.
instruction: Ensure adequate magnesium (300-400mg/day) when supplementing vitamin D3. Magnesium glycinate or malate are best absorbed.
mechanism: Magnesium-dependent enzymes (25-hydroxylase and 1-alpha-hydroxylase) convert vitamin D to its active form.
source: Journal of the American Osteopathic Association

title: St. John's Wort + 5-HTP — serotonin syndrome risk
detail: Both increase serotonin levels. Combined, they can cause serotonin syndrome — a potentially life-threatening condition with symptoms including agitation, confusion, rapid heart rate, and high blood pressure.
instruction: Do NOT combine St. John's Wort and 5-HTP. This combination can cause serotonin syndrome. If you take either, avoid the other completely.
mechanism: Synergistic serotonergic effects — St. John's Wort inhibits serotonin reuptake, 5-HTP increases serotonin production.

title: Multiple blood-thinning supplements — additive effect
detail: Fish oil and vitamin E both have anticoagulant properties. Together they have an additive blood-thinning effect. Adding more blood-thinning supplements (ginkgo, garlic) compounds this further.
instruction: Be aware of the cumulative blood-thinning effect. Stop all blood-thinning supplements 2 weeks before surgery. Discuss with your doctor if you're on any anticoagulant medication.
mechanism: Fish oil reduces platelet aggregation; vitamin E inhibits platelet aggregation and vitamin K-dependent clotting factors.

title: Ginkgo + Fish Oil — combined blood-thinning
detail: Both ginkgo biloba and fish oil inhibit platelet aggregation. Together they have an additive anticoagulant effect that can increase bleeding risk.
instruction: Use caution combining these, especially before surgery or if on blood thinners. Limit fish oil to 1-2g/day if taking ginkgo.
mechanism: Ginkgolides in ginkgo inhibit platelet activating factor; EPA/DHA in fish oil reduce thromboxane A2.

title: Valerian + Melatonin — excessive sedation
detail: Both valerian and melatonin promote sleep and have sedative effects. Combined, they may cause excessive drowsiness, especially when also taking magnesium or L-theanine.
instruction: Avoid combining valerian and melatonin unless under medical supervision. Start with one supplement at a time to assess your response.
mechanism: Additive CNS depressant effects — valerian enhances GABA activity, melatonin acts on melatonin receptors to promote sleep.

title: Berberine stacks — hypoglycemia risk
detail: Berberine significantly lowers blood sugar (comparable to metformin). Combining with other blood-sugar-lowering supplements (chromium, alpha lipoic acid, cinnamon) compounds the effect and can cause hypoglycemia.
instruction: Monitor blood sugar closely when combining berberine with other glucose-lowering supplements. Start with lower doses and increase gradually. Especially dangerous with diabetes medications.
mechanism: Berberine activates AMPK and inhibits mitochondrial complex I. Additive effects with other insulin sensitizers.

title: CoQ10 + Fish Oil — enhanced bioavailability
detail: Fish oil's omega-3 fatty acids improve CoQ10 absorption and both support mitochondrial function and cardiovascular health.
instruction: Take CoQ10 with fish oil and a fat-containing meal for best absorption. This is a beneficial combination for cardiovascular and energy support.
mechanism: Omega-3s improve the lipid environment for CoQ10 absorption. Both support mitochondrial membrane function.

title: Vitamin D3 enhances calcium absorption — take together
detail: Vitamin D3 is required for calcium absorption. Without adequate vitamin D3, only 10-15% of dietary calcium is absorbed. Together absorption rises to 30-40%.
instruction: Take vitamin D3 and calcium together with a fat-containing meal for maximum benefit. This is the ideal combination.
mechanism: Vitamin D3 upregulates TRPV6 calcium channels and calbindin in intestinal cells.

title: Probiotics must be separated from antibiotics
detail: Antibiotics kill the beneficial bacteria in probiotics if taken together. Probiotics must be taken at least 2 hours away from antibiotic doses to survive.
instruction: Take probiotics at least 2 hours after each antibiotic dose. Continue probiotics for 2-4 weeks after finishing antibiotics to restore gut flora.
mechanism: Antibiotics are bactericidal — they kill the live bacteria in probiotic supplements if taken simultaneously.

title: Ashwagandha + Magnesium — enhanced stress relief
detail: Both ashwagandha and magnesium support the stress response and HPA axis regulation. Magnesium is depleted by chronic stress. Combined they provide complementary cortisol-modulating effects.
instruction: This is a beneficial combination for stress and sleep. Take both in the evening. Magnesium glycinate is best for this purpose.
mechanism: Ashwagandha modulates cortisol via HPA axis; magnesium regulates NMDA receptors and supports parasympathetic tone.
source: Journal of the International Society of Sports Nutrition

title: Vitamin A toxicity from multiple sources
detail: Preformed vitamin A (retinol) accumulates in the liver and is toxic at high doses. Many multivitamins contain vitamin A — adding a separate vitamin A supplement can easily push intake to toxic levels.
instruction: Check your multivitamin for vitamin A content before adding a separate supplement. Total preformed vitamin A should not exceed 10,000 IU/day for most adults.
mechanism: Vitamin A is fat-soluble and stored in the liver. Unlike water-soluble vitamins, excess cannot be excreted and accumulates to toxic levels.

title: Zinc required to mobilize Vitamin A
detail: Zinc is needed to produce retinol-binding protein which transports vitamin A in the blood. Zinc deficiency can cause functional vitamin A deficiency even with adequate vitamin A intake.
instruction: Ensure adequate zinc (8-11mg/day) when supplementing vitamin A. This is especially important for immune function.
mechanism: Zinc is a cofactor for retinol dehydrogenase and retinol-binding protein synthesis.

title: Green tea reduces iron absorption
detail: Tannins in green tea bind to non-heme iron and can reduce absorption by up to 75%. Relevant for people with iron deficiency taking green tea extract or drinking green tea.
instruction: Take iron supplements at least 1 hour before or 2 hours after green tea or green tea extract. Take iron with vitamin C instead to maximize absorption.
mechanism: Polyphenols and tannins in green tea form insoluble complexes with iron.

title: Vitamin C regenerates Vitamin E
detail: Vitamin C regenerates oxidized vitamin E back to its active antioxidant form. Together they provide synergistic antioxidant protection.
instruction: Taking vitamins C and E together enhances the antioxidant benefits of both. Take vitamin E with a fat-containing meal.
mechanism: Ascorbic acid reduces tocopheroxyl radical back to alpha-tocopherol, extending vitamin E's antioxidant activity.

title: Melatonin + 5-HTP — excessive sedation and serotonin effects
detail: 5-HTP is a serotonin precursor that also increases melatonin synthesis. Combined with melatonin supplementation, total melatonin effects may be excessive.
instruction: Avoid combining high-dose melatonin with 5-HTP. If using both, use low doses of each and do not take during the day.
mechanism: 5-HTP → serotonin → melatonin pathway means 5-HTP supplementation already raises melatonin levels.

title: ALA + CoQ10 — mitochondrial synergy
detail: Alpha lipoic acid and CoQ10 both support mitochondrial energy production and have complementary antioxidant effects. ALA also regenerates other antioxidants including vitamin C and E.
instruction: This is a beneficial combination for energy and cellular health. Take both with a fat-containing meal. R-form ALA is more bioavailable than racemic.
mechanism: CoQ10 is in the electron transport chain; ALA is a cofactor for pyruvate dehydrogenase. Together they optimize ATP production.
source: Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences

title: Fiber supplements reduce absorption of many supplements and medications
detail: Psyllium husk and other soluble fiber supplements bind to many supplements and medications and reduce their absorption. This includes fat-soluble vitamins, minerals, and many drugs.
instruction: Take fiber supplements at least 2 hours apart from other supplements and medications. Take fiber with plenty of water.
mechanism: Soluble fiber forms a gel that binds to other compounds and reduces their intestinal absorption.

title: NAC + Glycine — GlyNAC combination for glutathione
detail: NAC and glycine are the two precursors to glutathione (the body's master antioxidant). Combined (GlyNAC) they raise glutathione more effectively than either alone.
instruction: Take NAC and glycine together — this is the clinically studied GlyNAC combination. Ratio of approximately 1:1 by weight.
mechanism: Glutathione = glycine + cysteine (from NAC) + glutamate. Both precursors are needed for synthesis.

title: St. John's Wort reduces melatonin effectiveness
detail: St. John's Wort induces CYP enzymes that metabolize melatonin faster, reducing its effectiveness. It also has its own effect on the sleep-wake cycle.
instruction: If taking St. John's Wort, melatonin may be less effective. Consider other sleep supports or adjust timing.
mechanism: St. John's Wort induces CYP1A2 which metabolizes melatonin, reducing its half-life and effectiveness.

title: Lion's Mane + Bacopa — complementary nootropic stack
detail: Lion's Mane supports NGF (nerve growth factor) and neurogenesis. Bacopa enhances memory consolidation and reduces anxiety. Together they provide complementary cognitive enhancement.
instruction: This is a well-regarded nootropic combination. Take bacopa with fat-containing meal (it's fat-soluble). Effects build over 8-12 weeks of consistent use.
mechanism: Lion's Mane increases BDNF and NGF; Bacopa inhibits AChE and modulates serotonin and dopamine.

title: Calcium severely reduces thyroid medication absorption
detail: Calcium supplements bind to levothyroxine and can reduce its absorption by 20-40%. This can cause hypothyroid symptoms even at the correct dose.
instruction: Take thyroid medication on an empty stomach, 30-60 minutes before breakfast. Take calcium at least 4 hours later.
mechanism: Calcium forms insoluble complexes with levothyroxine in the GI tract.

title: Berberine + Milk Thistle — liver protection
detail: Berberine is metabolized by the liver and can stress liver enzymes at high doses. Milk thistle (silymarin) protects liver cells and may enhance berberine's metabolic benefits.
instruction: Adding milk thistle when taking berberine long-term is a sensible protective measure. Take berberine with meals to reduce GI side effects.
mechanism: Silymarin protects hepatocytes via antioxidant and anti-inflammatory mechanisms while berberine activates AMPK for metabolic benefits.

title: High-dose fat-soluble vitamins — toxicity risk
detail: Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) accumulate in the body and can reach toxic levels. High-dose vitamin D and A together can cause hypercalcemia, bone loss, and liver toxicity.
instruction: Do not take megadoses of fat-soluble vitamins without medical supervision. Have blood levels checked before and during high-dose supplementation.
mechanism: Fat-soluble vitamins are stored in liver and adipose tissue. Unlike water-soluble vitamins, excess cannot be excreted and accumulates.

title: Creatine + Beta-Alanine — performance synergy
detail: Creatine enhances ATP production for short-burst power; beta-alanine buffers lactic acid for endurance. Together they cover complementary energy systems for athletic performance.
instruction: Take creatine (3-5g/day) and beta-alanine (3.2-6.4g/day) at any time — timing is not critical. Beta-alanine causes harmless skin tingling at first.
mechanism: Creatine replenishes phosphocreatine stores; beta-alanine is a carnosine precursor that buffers H+ ions during high-intensity exercise.

title: Iron and zinc compete — take separately
detail: High-dose iron and zinc compete for intestinal absorption via shared DMT1 transporters. Taking them together reduces absorption of both.
instruction: Separate iron and zinc by at least 2 hours. Take iron in the morning on an empty stomach with vitamin C, and zinc with an evening meal.
mechanism: Iron and zinc share intestinal DMT1 (divalent metal transporter 1) for absorption.

title: Quercetin + Bromelain — enhanced absorption
detail: Bromelain from pineapple significantly increases quercetin absorption. Many quercetin supplements include bromelain for this reason.
instruction: Look for quercetin supplements that include bromelain. If separate, take together. Quercetin + bromelain also provides complementary anti-inflammatory effects.
mechanism: Bromelain is a protease that increases GI permeability and quercetin transport across intestinal cells.

title: Kava + Alcohol — severe liver toxicity risk
detail: Kava alone carries liver toxicity risk. Combined with alcohol, the hepatotoxic risk is dramatically increased. Both are metabolized by similar liver pathways.
instruction: Never combine kava with alcohol. Avoid kava if you drink regularly or have any liver condition. Limit kava use to less than 3 months continuously.
mechanism: Both kava lactones and alcohol are hepatotoxic through oxidative stress and CYP2E1 pathways. Combination is synergistically toxic.

title: Phosphatidylserine + Omega-3 — brain health synergy
detail: Omega-3 fatty acids (especially DHA) are incorporated into phosphatidylserine-containing cell membranes. Together they provide synergistic support for brain cell membrane integrity and cognitive function.
instruction: Take phosphatidylserine with fish oil for enhanced brain health benefits. Both are fat-soluble — take with a meal containing fat.
mechanism: DHA is the preferred fatty acid incorporated into phosphatidylserine in neuronal membranes, enhancing membrane fluidity and receptor function.

title: Magnesium reduces iron absorption
detail: Magnesium competes with iron for intestinal absorption. Taking high-dose magnesium with iron reduces iron absorption significantly.
instruction: Separate magnesium and iron by at least 2 hours. Take iron in the morning and magnesium in the evening.
mechanism: Magnesium and iron share divalent metal transporter pathways in the intestine.

title: Resveratrol + Quercetin — synergistic senolytic stack
detail: Resveratrol and quercetin have complementary mechanisms for clearing senescent cells and activating longevity pathways (SIRT1, AMPK). Together they are a foundational longevity combination.
instruction: This is a well-studied longevity combination. Take with fat for absorption. Both are best taken with a meal.
mechanism: Resveratrol activates SIRT1 and inhibits mTOR; quercetin activates AMPK and has senolytic properties via BCL-2 inhibition.

title: Ephedra + Caffeine — serious cardiovascular risk
detail: The ephedra-caffeine combination causes dangerous increases in heart rate and blood pressure. This combination has been linked to heart attacks, strokes, and deaths and is banned in many countries.
instruction: Never combine ephedra (ma huang) with caffeine or caffeine-containing supplements. This combination is extremely dangerous and potentially fatal.
mechanism: Synergistic sympathomimetic effects — ephedra and caffeine both stimulate the sympathetic nervous system causing additive cardiovascular stress.

title: B12 and Folate — essential partnership
detail: Vitamin B12 and folate work together in methylation cycles. Folate supplementation without B12 can mask B12 deficiency. Both are needed for proper homocysteine metabolism.
instruction: Always ensure adequate B12 when supplementing folate, especially in older adults. Use methylcobalamin B12 and methylfolate (5-MTHF) for best results.
mechanism: B12 is needed to convert 5-methylTHF back to THF for DNA synthesis. Without B12, folate becomes trapped as 5-methylTHF (folate trap).

title: High-dose niacin + statins — myopathy risk
detail: High-dose niacin (>1g/day) combined with statin medications increases the risk of myopathy (muscle damage) and rhabdomyolysis. This is a serious drug-supplement interaction.
instruction: Avoid high-dose niacin supplements if taking any statin medication. Low-dose niacin (flush-free or nicotinamide form) at normal supplement doses is generally safer but still discuss with your doctor.
mechanism: Both high-dose niacin and statins can independently cause myopathy. The mechanism of their interaction is not fully understood but risk is additive.
source: Journal of the American College of Cardiology

title: Selenium + Vitamin E — synergistic antioxidants
detail: Selenium (as selenocysteine) is part of glutathione peroxidase which works alongside vitamin E to protect cells from oxidative damage. Together they are more effective than either alone.
instruction: A small amount of selenium (55-200mcg/day) enhances vitamin E's antioxidant effects. Do not exceed 400mcg/day selenium — it becomes toxic at high doses.
mechanism: Selenium-dependent glutathione peroxidase reduces lipid hydroperoxides; vitamin E prevents their formation. Complementary antioxidant defense.

title: Coffee reduces iron absorption
detail: Polyphenols in coffee (chlorogenic acid) bind to non-heme iron and can reduce absorption by 39-90% when consumed together.
instruction: Take iron supplements at least 1 hour before or 2 hours after coffee. Take with vitamin C to maximize absorption.
mechanism: Chlorogenic acid and other coffee polyphenols form insoluble chelates with iron.

title: NMN + Resveratrol — NAD+ longevity stack
detail: NMN raises NAD+ levels which are required for SIRT1 activation. Resveratrol activates SIRT1 but needs adequate NAD+ to function. Together they form a synergistic longevity combination studied by David Sinclair.
instruction: Take NMN in the morning (250-500mg) with resveratrol (250-500mg) and a fat source. This is a well-regarded longevity stack.
mechanism: NMN → NAD+; resveratrol activates sirtuins which require NAD+ as a cofactor. Resveratrol cannot activate sirtuins without adequate NAD+.

title: Iron and Vitamin E — take separately
detail: Iron and vitamin E should not be taken together. Iron can oxidize vitamin E and reduce its effectiveness. Vitamin E at high doses may also reduce iron absorption.
instruction: Separate iron and vitamin E by at least 8 hours. Take iron in the morning and vitamin E in the evening with dinner.
mechanism: Iron acts as a pro-oxidant in the presence of vitamin E, converting it to its oxidized form.

title: L-Theanine + Caffeine — smooth energy and focus
detail: L-theanine smooths caffeine's stimulant effects, reducing jitteriness and anxiety while maintaining alertness and focus. This is one of the most studied and reliable nootropic combinations.
instruction: Classic ratio is 2:1 theanine to caffeine (e.g. 200mg theanine + 100mg caffeine). Found naturally in green tea or take as supplements.
mechanism: L-theanine increases alpha brain waves and GABA, counteracting caffeine's anxiogenic effects while both enhance attention.

title: Fat-soluble vitamins require dietary fat for absorption
detail: Vitamins A, D, E, and K are fat-soluble and require dietary fat for absorption. Taking them with a fat-free meal or on an empty stomach dramatically reduces absorption.
instruction: Always take fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) with a meal containing fat. Even a small amount of fat (avocado, nuts, olive oil) significantly improves absorption.
mechanism: Fat-soluble vitamins are incorporated into micelles with dietary fat for intestinal absorption via chylomicrons.

title: Spirulina + Chlorella — complementary algae stack
detail: Spirulina is rich in protein and phycocyanin; chlorella is rich in chlorophyll and has chelating properties. Together they provide complementary nutritional and detoxification support.
instruction: This is a complementary combination. Chlorella's cell wall must be broken to be bioavailable — check for 'broken cell wall' on the label.
mechanism: Complementary nutrient profiles — spirulina for protein/antioxidants, chlorella for chlorophyll and heavy metal chelation.

title: Ginkgo + St. John's Wort — serotonin and bleeding risk
detail: Both ginkgo and St. John's Wort affect serotonin and both have anticoagulant properties. Combined they may increase both serotonergic effects and bleeding risk.
instruction: Use caution combining ginkgo and St. John's Wort. Do not add either to an SSRI regimen without medical supervision.
mechanism: Additive serotonergic effects and anticoagulant properties from both compounds.

title: Magnesium + Vitamin B6 — enhanced cellular uptake
detail: Vitamin B6 enhances magnesium transport into cells, improving the effectiveness of magnesium supplementation. This combination is especially relevant for stress, anxiety, and PMS.
instruction: Look for magnesium supplements that include B6 (P5P form is best). Or take B6 separately with your magnesium supplement.
mechanism: B6 (pyridoxal-5-phosphate) facilitates magnesium uptake into cells and is a cofactor for hundreds of enzymatic reactions that require magnesium.

title: High-dose zinc and copper ratio must be maintained
detail: Zinc and copper are antagonists — high zinc depletes copper and vice versa. The ratio matters: aim for 10:1 zinc to copper. Taking high doses of either disrupts this balance.
instruction: Maintain approximately 10:1 zinc to copper ratio. If taking 30mg zinc, take 3mg copper. Never take high-dose copper without monitoring — copper toxicity is serious.
mechanism: Metallothionein induced by zinc sequesters copper in intestinal cells, preventing its absorption.

title: Vitamin C required for collagen synthesis
detail: Vitamin C is an essential cofactor for prolyl hydroxylase and lysyl hydroxylase — the enzymes that stabilize collagen triple helix structure. Collagen supplements without adequate vitamin C are less effective.
instruction: Always take collagen with vitamin C (200-500mg). Many collagen supplements now include vitamin C for this reason.
mechanism: Vitamin C is required for hydroxylation of proline and lysine residues in collagen, which are essential for collagen stability.

title: High-dose Vitamin C reduces copper absorption
detail: Very high doses of vitamin C (>1500mg/day) can reduce copper absorption by converting cupric copper to cuprous form which is less absorbed.
instruction: If taking high-dose vitamin C long-term, ensure adequate copper intake (900mcg/day) and consider periodic copper level monitoring.
mechanism: Ascorbic acid reduces Cu2+ to Cu1+, the form that is less efficiently absorbed from the intestine.

title: Rhodiola + Ashwagandha — adaptogen stack
detail: Rhodiola is energizing and cognitive-enhancing; ashwagandha is calming and cortisol-reducing. Together they provide balanced adaptogenic support — energy without anxiety, calm without sedation.
instruction: Take rhodiola in the morning (energizing) and ashwagandha in the evening (calming). Effects build over 4-8 weeks of consistent use.
mechanism: Rhodiola activates stress response proteins and increases serotonin/dopamine; ashwagandha modulates GABA receptors and reduces cortisol.

title: 5-HTP + SSRI antidepressants — serotonin syndrome
detail: 5-HTP increases serotonin production while SSRIs prevent serotonin reuptake. Combined, serotonin accumulates to dangerous levels causing serotonin syndrome.
instruction: NEVER combine 5-HTP with any SSRI (sertraline, fluoxetine, escitalopram, etc.) or SNRI medication. This can cause life-threatening serotonin syndrome.
mechanism: 5-HTP → serotonin (increased production) + SSRI blocks reuptake = dramatically elevated synaptic serotonin → toxidrome.

title: Fish oil enhances Vitamin D absorption
detail: Vitamin D3 is fat-soluble and its absorption is significantly enhanced when taken with dietary fat. Fish oil provides an excellent fat source and also has complementary cardiovascular benefits.
instruction: Take vitamin D3 with fish oil and a meal for maximum absorption. This is a commonly recommended combination for cardiovascular and immune health.
mechanism: Fat-soluble vitamin D3 is incorporated into chylomicrons with dietary fats (including omega-3s) for absorption.
source: Journal of the Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics

title: Melatonin + Alcohol — impaired sleep quality
detail: Alcohol disrupts melatonin production and its effects. Combined, they may cause excessive sedation initially but worsen overall sleep quality and cause next-day grogginess.
instruction: Avoid combining melatonin with alcohol. If you drink in the evening, skip melatonin that night.
mechanism: Alcohol suppresses melatonin secretion and disrupts circadian rhythm. Combined sedative effects then rebound wakefulness.

title: Curcumin + Boswellia — synergistic anti-inflammatory
detail: Curcumin and boswellia (frankincense) target different inflammatory pathways — curcumin inhibits NF-kB and COX-2; boswellia inhibits 5-LOX. Together they provide broader anti-inflammatory coverage than either alone.
instruction: This is a well-studied joint and inflammation combination. Take both with food and fat for absorption. Curcumin needs piperine or enhanced delivery form.
mechanism: Curcumin: NF-kB and COX-2 inhibition. Boswellia: 5-LOX inhibition and leukotriene reduction. Non-overlapping anti-inflammatory pathways.

title: Selenium toxicity — narrow therapeutic window
detail: Selenium has one of the narrowest safe ranges of any mineral. The difference between beneficial (55-200mcg/day) and toxic (>400mcg/day) doses is small. Multiple selenium-containing supplements can easily push intake to toxic levels.
instruction: Check all supplements for selenium content (brazil nuts, multivitamins, thyroid formulas all contain selenium). Total daily intake should not exceed 400mcg. Symptoms of toxicity include hair loss, nail brittleness, and garlic breath.
mechanism: Excess selenium incorporates into proteins replacing sulfur-containing amino acids, disrupting protein function.

title: Ginger + Turmeric — enhanced anti-inflammatory
detail: Ginger and turmeric both have anti-inflammatory effects but through different mechanisms. Ginger also improves turmeric absorption and adds digestive benefits.
instruction: This is a classic Ayurvedic combination. Take with black pepper and fat for maximum curcumin absorption. Both have mild blood-thinning effects — note if on anticoagulants.
mechanism: Ginger's gingerols inhibit COX and LOX enzymes; turmeric's curcuminoids inhibit NF-kB. Complementary and additive anti-inflammatory action.

title: High-dose Vitamin B6 — peripheral neuropathy risk
detail: Vitamin B6 is toxic at high doses (>100mg/day long-term). Many supplements contain 50-100mg per serving — stacking B6-containing supplements can easily exceed safe limits. Symptoms: tingling and numbness in hands and feet.
instruction: Check all B-complex and multivitamin supplements for B6 content. Keep total B6 under 50mg/day for long-term use. If experiencing tingling in extremities, stop all B6 supplements immediately.
mechanism: Excess pyridoxine accumulates and causes sensory neuropathy through unclear mechanisms, possibly by inhibiting pyridoxal-dependent enzymes.
